Harun al-Rashid dies. Al-Ma'mun's half-brother, al-Amin, becomes caliph in Baghdad, while al-Ma'mun governs the eastern province of Khurasan.
The Fourth Fitna, a civil war between al-Amin and al-Ma'mun, culminates in the Siege of Baghdad and al-Ma'mun's victory.
Founds the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad, a major center for translation and research.
Sponsors an expedition of astronomers to measure the Earth's circumference, achieving a remarkably accurate result for the time.
Initiates the 'Mihna' (inquisition) to enforce Mu'tazilite doctrine. Dies during a military campaign against the Byzantine Empire.
Founding the House of Wisdom
Established a grand library and academy in Baghdad that became the intellectual heart of the Islamic Golden Age.
The Great Translation Movement
Sponsored the translation of countless Greek, Persian, and Indian scientific and philosophical works into Arabic.
Patronage of Science
Funded major scientific projects, including astronomical observations and the first algebraic text by al-Khwarizmi.
What was the House of Wisdom?

It was a major public academy, intellectual center, and library in Baghdad during the Islamic Golden Age. Scholars from diverse backgrounds gathered there to translate, research, and expand upon world knowledge.
Why was Al-Ma'mun's reign considered a 'Golden Age'?

His active patronage of science, philosophy, medicine, and the arts led to an unprecedented flourishing of knowledge and culture. His reign saw major advancements in algebra, astronomy, and medicine, building upon the works of ancient civilizations.
What was the 'Mihna'?

The Mihna, or 'ordeal,' was an inquisition started by al-Ma'mun in 833 to impose the Mu'tazilite school of thought, which held that the Qur'an was created and not co-eternal with God. He persecuted traditionalist scholars who disagreed, most famously Ahmad ibn Hanbal.
Born in Baghdad, Abbasid Caliphate, a major center of learning.
Begins his extensive travels, which would last for over two decades and cover vast regions of Asia and Africa.
Travels through Persia and into the Indus Valley in India, documenting local customs and history.
Completes the first version of his major work, 'Muruj al-Dhahab' (The Meadows of Gold).
Settles in Fustat (Old Cairo), Egypt, where he spends his later years writing and revising his works.
Dies in Fustat, Egypt, leaving behind a monumental but largely lost body of work.
The Meadows of Gold and Mines of Gems
Authored this encyclopedic work combining history, geography, and ethnography, departing from a purely chronological to a thematic approach.
Pioneering Ethnography
Documented the customs, beliefs, and social structures of diverse peoples he encountered, based on direct observation and interviews.
Integration of Sciences
Combined historical narratives with scientific discussions on geology, zoology, and astronomy, creating a holistic view of the world.
Why is he called the 'Herodotus of the Arabs'?

Like the Greek historian Herodotus, Al-Masudi traveled extensively and wrote a comprehensive history that included geography, culture, and personal observations, going beyond simple chronicles of rulers and wars.
How much of his work survived?

Unfortunately, very little. Of his estimated 34 works, only two major ones survive: 'The Meadows of Gold' and 'Kitab al-Tanbih wa-l-Ishraf' (The Book of Notification and Revision). The rest, including a 30-volume universal history, are lost.
Did he really travel to China?

While he wrote extensively about China, it is debated among scholars whether he personally visited mainland China or gathered his information from sailors and merchants he met in ports in India, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia. He certainly reached the China Sea.
Born in Harran, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Turkey).
Late 9th Century
Moved to Raqqa, Syria, and began his major astronomical observations.
Completed his magnum opus, the Kitāb az-Zīj aṣ-Ṣābi’ (The Sabian Astronomical Tables).
His work was first translated into Latin by Plato of Tivoli as 'De Motu Stellarum' (On the Motion of the Stars).
Died near Samarra, Iraq, while returning from a trip to Baghdad.
Solar Year Calculation
Calculated the solar year to be 365 days, 5 hours, 46 minutes, and 24 seconds, only 2 minutes and 22 seconds off the modern value.
Trigonometric Innovations
Introduced and popularized the use of sine, cosine, and tangent functions, replacing the Greek system of chords.
Refining Precession
Determined the precession of the equinoxes to be 1 degree every 66 years, a significant improvement on Ptolemy's value of 1 degree per 100 years.
Kitāb az-Zīj
Authored a comprehensive book of 57 astronomical tables that greatly influenced Islamic and European astronomy for centuries.
Why is he called the 'Ptolemy of the Arabs'?

This title recognizes his immense stature in astronomy, comparable to Ptolemy in the ancient world, and highlights his role in significantly advancing and correcting Ptolemy's work within the Islamic tradition.
Did Al-Battani use a telescope?
No, telescopes were not invented until the 17th century. Al-Battani made his remarkably accurate observations using instruments like the armillary sphere, mural quadrant, and astrolabe.
How did his work reach Europe?

His major work, Kitāb az-Zīj, was translated into Latin in the 12th century as 'De Scientia Stellarum' or 'De Motu Stellarum', becoming a foundational text for Renaissance astronomers like Copernicus.
What is a 'Zīj'?

A Zīj is an Islamic astronomical book that tabulates parameters used for astronomical calculations of the positions of the Sun, Moon, stars, and planets. Al-Battani's Zīj was one of the most accurate and influential of its time.
Born in Basra (modern-day Iraq).
Moves to Baghdad and begins to gain recognition in the intellectual circles of the Abbasid Caliphate.
Composes his major works, including the 'Book of Animals' (Kitab al-Hayawan) and the 'Book of Eloquence and Exposition' (Kitab al-Bayan wa-al-Tabyin).
Is briefly appointed to an office in the Caliph's court by al-Mutawakkil, but resigns after only three days.
Dies in his hometown of Basra at an advanced age.
Book of Animals (Kitab al-Hayawan)
A monumental work of zoology, literature, and philosophy, containing early concepts of natural selection and food chains.
Master of Arabic Prose
Elevated Arabic prose to a high art form, influencing generations of writers with his clear, witty, and anecdotal style.
Pioneering Polymath
Contributed to a vast range of subjects including theology, sociology, politics, and literary criticism, embodying the spirit of the Islamic Golden Age.
Did Al-Jahiz actually propose the theory of evolution?

He proposed concepts like the struggle for existence, adaptation, and environmental influence on animals, which are foundational to evolutionary theory, but he did not formulate a complete theory of evolution like Charles Darwin.
What does the name 'Al-Jahiz' mean?
It is a nickname meaning 'the goggle-eyed' or 'the bug-eyed', reportedly given to him due to a congenital deformity of his eyes.
How did Al-Jahiz die?
The exact cause is unknown, but a popular, though likely apocryphal, story claims he was killed when a pile of books fell on him in his private library.
Born in Kufa, Abbasid Caliphate, to an aristocratic family of the Kindah tribe.
Flourished in Baghdad under Caliph al-Ma'mun, working at the House of Wisdom to translate and study Greek philosophical and scientific texts.
Continued his scholarly work under the patronage of Caliph al-Mu'tasim.
Fell out of favor during the reign of Caliph al-Mutawakkil. He faced persecution, and his personal library was temporarily confiscated.
His library and favor were restored, allowing him to resume his work.
Died in Baghdad, leaving a legacy of over 260 written works.
Father of Islamic Philosophy
He was the first to systematically introduce and synthesize Greek philosophical concepts into the Islamic intellectual tradition.
Pioneer of Cryptanalysis
He authored one of the earliest known books on cryptanalysis, introducing the method of frequency analysis to break ciphers.
Polymath Contributions
He wrote extensively on metaphysics, ethics, mathematics, medicine, music theory, and optics, demonstrating a vast range of knowledge.
Music Theory
He was the first great theoretician of music in the Arab-Islamic world and proposed adding a fifth string to the 'ud (lute).

It was a major intellectual center during the Islamic Golden Age in Baghdad. Scholars from various backgrounds gathered there to translate, study, and preserve knowledge from across the world, particularly from Greek, Persian, and Indian sources.
Why was Al-Kindi's library confiscated?

It was likely due to a combination of court intrigues and theological disputes. Al-Kindi was associated with the rationalist Mu'tazilite school, which fell out of favor with the more orthodox Caliph al-Mutawakkil, and his rivals used this to discredit him.
What is frequency analysis in cryptography?

It is a cryptanalytic technique, pioneered by Al-Kindi for code-breaking, that involves analyzing how often letters or groups of letters appear in a ciphertext. The method works because certain letters (like 'E' in English) appear more frequently than others in any given language.
Did Al-Kindi only focus on Greek philosophy?

No, while he is famous for introducing Greek philosophy, he was a polymath who also made significant contributions to mathematics, medicine, optics, music theory, and was a pioneer in the field of cryptanalysis.
Born in Amol, Tabaristan (modern-day Mazandaran Province, Iran).
Left home at age 12 to study in Rayy, then traveled to Baghdad.
Traveled extensively through Syria, Palestine, and Egypt to collect hadith and historical reports.
Settled permanently in Baghdad, where he spent the rest of his life teaching and writing.
Completed his monumental history, 'Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk' (History of the Prophets and Kings).
Faced persecution from the Hanbali school in Baghdad, leading to a form of house arrest.
Died in Baghdad at the age of approximately 83-84.
History of the Prophets and Kings
A massive and detailed annalistic world history from creation to the year 915, preserving countless earlier sources.
Tafsir al-Tabari
One of the most comprehensive and influential early commentaries on the Quran, compiling a vast range of interpretations.
Jariri Madhhab
Founded his own, albeit short-lived, school of Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) that differed from the four main Sunni schools.
Why is his 'History of the Prophets and Kings' so important?
It is one of the most comprehensive primary sources for the first three centuries of Islamic history, meticulously preserving reports and sources that would have otherwise been lost.
What was al-Tabari's method as a historian?
He primarily used the isnad system, common in hadith studies. He cited his chain of transmitters for each historical report, allowing later scholars to assess the reliability of his sources.
Why did his school of law (madhhab) fail to survive?

It faced fierce opposition from the more established and politically powerful Hanbali school in Baghdad, which disagreed with some of his legal interpretations and actively worked to suppress his influence.
Was al-Tabari Sunni or Shia?

Al-Tabari is considered a Sunni scholar. While he founded his own school of jurisprudence, his theological and historical works are firmly within the Sunni tradition, though he is respected by scholars of all traditions for his immense erudition.
Born as Muhammad bin Dawud Chaghri.
Ascends to the throne of the Seljuk Empire after the death of his uncle, Tughril.
Captures Ani, the capital of Armenia, expanding Seljuk influence into the Caucasus.
Achieves a decisive victory over the Byzantine Empire at the Battle of Manzikert and captures Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes.
Mortally wounded by a prisoner during a campaign in Transoxiana and dies shortly after, succeeded by his son Malik-Shah I.
Victory at the Battle of Manzikert (1071)
Defeated a numerically superior Byzantine army, capturing Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes and opening Anatolia to Turkish migration.
Consolidation of the Seljuk Empire
Unified disparate Turkoman tribes and expanded the empire's territory, establishing it as the dominant power in the Middle East.
Patronage of Arts and Sciences
Under the guidance of his vizier Nizam al-Mulk, he supported the establishment of 'Nizamiyya' schools, fostering an era of intellectual growth.
Why is Alp Arslan called the 'Valiant Lion'?
Alp Arslan is a Turkic honorific name. 'Alp' means 'heroic' or 'brave,' and 'Arslan' means 'lion.' The name reflects his reputation as a fierce and courageous military leader.
Did he intend to conquer Anatolia?

Most historians believe his primary goal was to secure his borders and counter the Fatimid Caliphate in Egypt. The victory at Manzikert was an unexpected opportunity that led to the gradual Turkification of Anatolia, rather than a premeditated invasion.
How did Alp Arslan die?
During a campaign in Transoxiana, a captured fortress commander named Yussuf al-Khwarizmi, when brought before him for sentencing, lunged at the Sultan with a hidden dagger, mortally wounding him.
Born in Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul) to a wealthy family with connections to the Ottoman court.
Reportedly has a dream of the Prophet Muhammad, which he interprets as a divine sign to embark on a life of travel.
Begins his first major journey outside of Constantinople, marking the start of four decades of extensive travel.
Travels extensively through the Ottoman Empire's territories in Anatolia, Rumelia, the Levant, Egypt, and neighboring lands including Persia, Austria, and Russia.
Dies, though the exact date and place are unknown. He is believed to have died in Constantinople or Cairo, having completed his 10-volume Seyahatname.
The Seyahatname (Book of Travels)
Authored a monumental 10-volume travelogue detailing 40 years of journeys, providing an invaluable window into the 17th-century Ottoman world.
Pioneering Ethnographer and Linguist
Documented numerous languages, dialects, cultures, and social customs across a vast geographical area, many for the first time.
Comprehensive Geographic and Social Record
His work provides detailed descriptions of cities, architecture, trade routes, and social structures, making it a primary source for historians.
Was everything in the Seyahatname true?
Not entirely. While it's a vital historical source, Evliya often embellished stories, included tall tales, and sometimes reported hearsay as fact, blending reality with imaginative storytelling.
How did he fund his travels?
He was from a wealthy family and often traveled as part of official entourages for governors and pashas, serving as a companion, imam, or messenger, which provided him with protection and resources.
How large is the Seyahatname?
It consists of ten large volumes. Each volume typically covers a specific period or geographical region of his travels, such as Volume I on Istanbul or Volume X on Egypt and Sudan.
Born in Kolozsvár (now Cluj-Napoca, Romania).
Captured during a conflict and brought to the Ottoman Empire, where he converted to Islam.
Enters the Müteferrika corps, serving as a diplomat and envoy for the Sultan.
Submits his memorandum, 'Vesiletü't-Tıbâa' (The Usefulness of Printing), to the Grand Vizier.
Receives a fatwa and an imperial edict from Sultan Ahmed III, authorizing the printing of non-religious works.
Publishes the first book from his press, the 'Vankulu Lügati', a two-volume Arabic-Turkish dictionary.
Publishes Katip Çelebi's 'Cihannüma', a major work of geography, including new maps he engraved himself.
Dies in Constantinople (Istanbul). His press had printed 17 different titles by this time.
First Official Ottoman Printing Press
He established the first printing press with movable Arabic type officially sanctioned by the Ottoman state in 1727.
Publication of Key Secular Works
He printed 17 important works on history, geography, and language, including the famous 'Vankulu Lügati' dictionary and the 'Cihannüma' atlas.
Pioneer of Modern Cartography
He engraved and printed some of the first modern-style maps in the Ottoman Empire, updating geographical knowledge.
Why was printing with Arabic type introduced so late to the Ottoman Empire?

There was strong resistance from thousands of calligraphers who saw it as a threat to their livelihood, and religious conservatism that was wary of printing the Quran and other religious texts with a mechanical device.
Was Muteferrika the very first to print in the Ottoman Empire?

No. Non-Muslim communities (Jews, Armenians, Greeks) had operated their own presses for centuries, but Muteferrika's was the first press run by a Muslim and officially sanctioned by the Sultan to print in the Ottoman Turkish language using Arabic script.
What happened to his printing press after his death?
The press ceased operations shortly after his death in 1745 and was only revived several decades later, showing the personal drive and high-level patronage that was behind the initial venture.
Born in Kavala, Ottoman Rumelia (modern-day Greece).
Leads the Egyptian army in the Ottoman–Saudi War, defeating the First Saudi State.
Commands Egyptian forces in the Greek War of Independence, conquering the Morea.
His fleet is destroyed by a combined European force at the Battle of Navarino.
Leads the first Egyptian-Ottoman War, conquering Syria and advancing into Anatolia.
Decisively defeats the Ottoman army at the Battle of Konya.
Forced to withdraw from Syria by European powers under the Convention of London.
Becomes regent for his ailing father, but dies of tuberculosis after only a few months.
Modernization of the Egyptian Army
Transformed the Egyptian military into a modern, European-style fighting force, making it one of the most powerful in the region.
Conquest and Administration of Syria
Successfully invaded and governed Greater Syria for nearly a decade, implementing significant administrative and legal reforms.
Defeat of the First Saudi State
Led the campaign that crushed the Wahhabi-led First Saudi State and recaptured the holy cities of Mecca and Medina for the Ottoman Sultan.
Was Ibrahim Pasha the son or adopted son of Muhammad Ali?
While some early European sources claimed he was adopted, modern scholarship confirms he was Muhammad Ali's biological eldest son with his first wife.
Why did he fail to take Constantinople in 1832?
After his victory at Konya, the path to the Ottoman capital was open, but diplomatic pressure and military intervention from Russia, followed by Britain and France, forced him to halt his advance.
He died in November 1848 from tuberculosis, just a few months after being appointed regent and a year before the death of his father, Muhammad Ali.
Born in Córdoba, Al-Andalus (modern-day Spain).
Appointed qadi (judge) in Seville.
Became chief physician to the Almohad caliph Abu Yaqub Yusuf in Marrakesh.
Fell from political favor, was exiled to Lucena, and his philosophical works were banned and burned.
Recalled from exile and his honor was restored shortly before his death.
Died in Marrakesh, Morocco.
The Commentaries on Aristotle
He wrote extensive commentaries that re-introduced and clarified Aristotle's works for Islamic, Jewish, and Christian thinkers.
The Incoherence of the Incoherence
A detailed rebuttal to Al-Ghazali's critique of philosophy, defending the use of reason and Aristotelianism within an Islamic context.
Kulliyat fi al-Tibb (Generalities on Medicine)
A comprehensive medical encyclopedia that was widely used as a textbook in Europe for centuries, known in Latin as 'Colliget'.
Fasl al-Maqal (The Decisive Treatise)
A philosophical treatise arguing for the harmony and compatibility between Islamic scripture and Aristotelian philosophy.
Why was he called 'The Commentator'?

Dante Alighieri famously gave him this title in 'The Divine Comedy'. It was because his detailed analyses of Aristotle's works were considered the definitive interpretations in Europe for centuries, profoundly shaping medieval scholasticism.
What is the 'double truth' theory often associated with him?

It's the idea that one truth can be reached through philosophy (reason) and another through religion (revelation), and both can be valid even if they conflict. Averroes himself argued for a single truth accessible through different paths, but some of his more radical Latin followers developed this controversial concept.
Did he only write about philosophy?

No, he was a true polymath. He served as a respected judge (qadi) in Seville and Córdoba, was a court physician, and also wrote influential works on medicine, astronomy, mathematics, and Islamic jurisprudence.
Born near Bukhara, Transoxiana (present-day Uzbekistan).
Cures the Samanid emir Nuh ibn Mansur, gaining access to the royal library.
Begins a life of wandering after the fall of the Samanid dynasty.
Completes his major work, 'The Canon of Medicine' (Al-Qanun fi'l-Tibb).
Completes his philosophical encyclopedia, 'The Book of Healing' (Kitab al-Shifa).
Dies in Hamadan, Persia (present-day Iran).
The Canon of Medicine
Authored this five-volume medical encyclopedia which became a standard medical text in Europe and the Islamic world for centuries.
The Book of Healing
Wrote this monumental philosophical and scientific encyclopedia, covering logic, natural sciences, psychology, and metaphysics.
Pioneer of Aromatherapy
Systematized the use of essential oils for therapeutic purposes, inventing the process of steam distillation.
Synthesis of Philosophy
Integrated Aristotelianism with Islamic theology, creating a comprehensive philosophical system that influenced later Muslim and European thinkers.
Was Avicenna just a doctor?
No, he was a true polymath, making significant contributions to philosophy, astronomy, logic, mathematics, and poetry, in addition to medicine.
Is it true he memorized the Quran by age 10?
Historical accounts claim he was a child prodigy who had memorized the entire Quran by the age of 10.
Why is he called Avicenna?
"Avicenna" is the Latinized version of his Arabic patronymic, "Ibn Sina" (Son of Sina).
Born in Murcia, al-Andalus (modern-day Spain).
His family moves to Seville, a major center of culture and learning.
Undergoes a profound spiritual conversion and begins his journey on the Sufi path.
Leaves the Iberian Peninsula permanently, beginning his extensive travels.
Arrives in Mecca, where he has a series of visions and begins writing his magnum opus, 'The Meccan Illuminations' (Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya).
Settles permanently in Damascus, which becomes his base for teaching and writing.
Completes 'The Bezels of Wisdom' (Fusus al-Hikam), a concise and highly influential work on his mystical philosophy.
Dies in Damascus and is buried at the foot of Mount Qasioun.
The Meccan Illuminations (Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya)
His magnum opus, a monumental encyclopedia of Islamic esoteric sciences, metaphysics, and cosmology.
The Bezels of Wisdom (Fusus al-Hikam)
A concise summary of his mystical teachings on the nature of prophets, considered a cornerstone of Sufi thought.
Wahdat al-Wujud (The Unity of Being)
Articulated the influential metaphysical doctrine that all existence is a single, unified reality, a manifestation of God.
What does 'al-Shaykh al-Akbar' mean?
It means 'the Greatest Master' or 'the Greatest Sheikh' in Arabic. It is a title of reverence given to him by later generations of Sufis and scholars due to his immense influence.
